title: Endovascular Thrombectomy in Medium and Distal Vessel Occlusions: A Focused Guideline From the Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology Guidelines and Practice Standards Committee
one_line_summary: Society of Vascular and Interventional Neurology guideline providing territory-specific recommendations for endovascular thrombectomy in distal- and medium-vessel occlusion acute ischemic stroke, emphasizing a selective, evidence-weighted approach based on recent randomized trial data.
key_pearls: Routine EVT for all DMVO is not supported by current RCT evidence; a tailored approach based on vascular territory, stroke severity, and tissue at risk is **essential**.
EVT is reasonable (Class IIa) for disabling proximal/dominant M2 occlusions with moderate-to-severe deficits and salvageable brain tissue, based on HERMES and observational data.
In codominant/nondominant M2 occlusions with NIHSS ≥6, EVT may be considered (Class IIb) per ORIENTAL-MeVO trial results showing improved functional independence.
Routine EVT is not **recommended** (Class III) for M3/M4, A2/A3, and P1-P3 occlusions due to neutral or negative outcomes in ESCAPE-MeVO, DISTAL, and DISCOUNT trials.

The DISTALS trial demonstrated a 3-fold higher successful reperfusion rate without sICH (86.3% vs 27.7%, P<0.001) using the Tigertriever 13 adjustable stent retriever purpose-built for distal vessels.

Patient selection should prioritize clinical severity (NIHSS ≥6 or disabling deficit), limited infarct core, favorable collaterals, and rapid treatment eligibility, avoiding EVT in low-severity, nondisabling strokes.
Future trials and purpose-built devices (e.g., adjustable stentrievers, next-generation aspiration catheters) may improve the risk-benefit profile of DMVO EVT.
consensus_method: Expert panel consensus with final approval by the SVIN Board of Directors, following the SVIN Guidelines and Practice Standards Algorithm.

recommendation_blocks.topic: M2 Segment Occlusions (Middle Cerebral Artery Insular Segment)
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- M2 occlusions can cause disabling deficits, especially when involving a **dominant branch** (supplying >50% of MCA territory).
- HERMES pooled analysis: only 11% (130 patients) had M2 occlusions; 89% were proximal, 56% dominant. EVT was highly effective (90-day mRS 0-2: **adjusted OR 2.68 [95% CI 1.13–6.37]**), with dominant M2 occlusions benefiting substantially (**adjusted OR 4.08 [95% CI 1.08–15.48]**).
- ESCAPE-MeVO (n=530): primary outcome mRS 0-1 was **41.6% EVT vs 43.1% medical** (adjusted rate ratio 0.95 [95% CI 0.79–1.15]); higher **mortality** in EVT arm (**13.3% vs 8.4%**, adjusted HR 1.82 [95% CI 1.06–3.12]).
- DISTAL (n=543): no reduction in disability (excellent outcome OR 0.88 [95% CI 0.61–1.25]); 90-day **mortality** similar (**15.5% vs 14%**).
- DISCOUNT interim: mRS 0-2 was **60% EVT vs 77% medical**; trial stopped.
- ORIENTAL-MeVO (n=564, NIHSS ≥6): EVT **significantly** improved functional independence at 90 days (mRS 0-2: **58.6% vs 46.6%, P=0.009**); benefit driven by NIHSS ≥8. M2 subgroup (n=122) had strongest treatment effect (adjusted rate ratio **1.43 [95% CI 1.12–1.82]**).
- DISTALS trial: Tigertriever 13 achieved **86.3% successful reperfusion without sICH vs 27.7% medical (P<0.001)**, with zero sICH in per-protocol arm; enrolled only codominant/nondominant M2 (n=47).
- 2025 neutral trials predominantly enrolled nondominant M2 with low median NIHSS (e.g., **median NIHSS 6 in DISTAL**), potentially excluding severe dominant M2 strokes.
- sICH risk: ESCAPE-MeVO **5.4% EVT vs 2.2% medical**; DISCOUNT **12% vs 6%**.
- Subgroup analyses suggest time matters: trend toward better outcomes with EVT treated <4.5 hours.
- Technique: aspiration vs stent retriever show comparable efficacy in M2 (ASTER, COMPASS). Stent retrievers may have higher hemorrhagic complications in tortuous distal vessels; aspiration-first approach may be favorable for DMVO EVT.
- Vessel perforation incidence in DMVO: **4.8% overall**; higher in M3 (**8.9%**) and A2 (**8.3%**) vs M2 (**4.3%**).

recommendations.statement: EVT is reasonable for patients with disabling stroke due to proximal and/or dominant M2 occlusions, particularly when associated with moderate-to-severe deficits and imaging evidence of salvageable brain tissue.
EVT may be considered in distal, codominant, or nondominant M2 occlusions who present with disabling deficits and an NIHSS score ≥6.
Routine EVT is not **recommended** for patients with distal or nondominant M2 occlusions presenting with low stroke severity and nondisabling symptoms.
recommendations.evidence_grade: Class III: No Benefit; LOE A

recommendation_blocks.topic: M3/M4 Segments Occlusions (Distal MCA Cortical Branches)
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- M3/M4 occlusions typically produce smaller, focal strokes with mild-to-moderate deficits; some can be disabling (e.g., precentral branch causing dense hand weakness).
- DISTAL: 27% had M3/M4; subgroup analysis showed no functional benefit (adjusted OR **0.77 [95% CI 0.44–1.37]**).
- ESCAPE-MeVO: 41% M3 occlusions; no benefit (adjusted OR **1.01 [95% CI 0.77–1.31]**); sICH higher in EVT arm (**5.9% vs 2.6%**).
- DISCOUNT: 76% M3 occlusions; trend toward worse outcomes with EVT (mRS 0-2: **60% EVT vs 77% medical**); higher overall ICH rate (**44% vs 29%**).
- ORIENTAL-MeVO: M3 subgroup (n=54) did not show **significant** functional benefit.
- DISTALS: none of the 28 M3 patients had sICH.
- High rates of IV tPA in trials (60-70%).
- Decision-making should be case-by-case: consider NIHSS ≥5 threshold, eloquent deficit (aphasia, hemianopia, hemiparesis), infarct burden, and procedural risk.

recommendations.statement: EVT with mechanical devices is not **recommended** as a standard treatment for occlusions of the M3/M4 MCA branches.
recommendations.evidence_grade: Class III: No Benefit; LOE B-R

recommendations.statement: It is reasonable to enroll patients with M3/M4 occlusions and disabling deficit in future RCTs examining the safety and efficacy of EVT using aspiration technique, small/adjustable stentrievers, or intra-arterial thrombolysis compared with best medical management.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Isolated ACA occlusions beyond A1 are uncommon (≈1-5% of AIS) but can cause **significant** disability (leg weakness, abulia, incontinence).
- DISTAL: 30 patients (5.5%) with A2/A3; no benefit (OR **0.43 [95% CI 0.10–1.83]**).
- ESCAPE-MeVO: 24 patients (4.5%) with ACA; no subgroup outcomes reported.
- DISCOUNT: 27 patients (17%) with ACA; no subgroup results.
- ORIENTAL-MeVO: 66 patients with ACA; trend toward improved outcomes (adjusted rate ratio **1.23 [95% CI 0.93–1.69]**).
- DISTALS: 10 patients with ACA; no sICH.
- TOPMOST study: 110 matched patients; favorable outcome (mRS 0-2) **49% in both groups**; excellent outcome (mRS 0-1) **37.8% EVT vs 33.3% medical**; **mortality** **21.6% EVT vs 30.8% medical (P>0.05)**; successful recanalization **81%**; sICH **2.1%**.
- MAD-MT: procedural success **91%** without substantial improvement in functional outcome over BMT.
- Many ACA deficits are behavioral (abulia, agitation, emotional lability, cognitive decline) not captured by mRS.

recommendations.statement: In acute ischemic stroke due to an occlusion of the distal ACA (A2/A3 segments), routine EVT is not **recommended** due to insufficient evidence demonstrating clear benefit.
recommendations.evidence_grade: Class III: No Benefit; LOE B-NR

recommendations.statement: In patients with ACA occlusion with disabling deficits, large territory at risk, favorable imaging, and good baseline functional status, EVT may be considered on a case-by-case basis.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Isolated PCA occlusions most often present with visual field deficits, cortical blindness, or alexia without agraphia; proximal P1 can cause midbrain/thalamic syndromes.
- 2025 DMVO RCTs: no functional benefit of EVT for P2/P3 subgroups.
- ORIENTAL-MeVO: 54 patients with PCA; trend towards benefit (adjusted rate ratio **1.37 [95% CI 0.95–2.00]**).
- DISTALS: 32 patients with PCA; improved successful reperfusion and no sICH.
- PLATO study: no improvement in disability outcomes with EVT vs medical; EVT associated with greater early NIHSS improvement and higher rates of complete visual recovery.
- TOPMOST registry: possible advantage in patients with more severe strokes (NIHSS ≥10) or ineligible for IV thrombolysis.
- Meta-analyses: no improvement in functional outcomes; potential increase in **mortality** among EVT-treated patients.
- mRS may not fully capture disability/recovery in PCA strokes.

recommendations.statement: EVT is not **recommended** as a routine treatment for isolated PCA occlusions.
recommendations.evidence_grade: Class III: No Benefit; LOE B-NR

recommendations.statement: EVT for isolated PCA occlusions (P1, P2, or P3 segments) may be considered in selected patients who present early, exhibit severe neurological deficits **significantly** impacting quality of life, or who are ineligible for intravenous thrombolysis.

bedside_protocol.title: Assess Clinical Severity & Territory
bedside_protocol.action: Determine if the DMVO involves a dominant/proximal M2 with disabling deficit (NIHSS ≥6 or eloquent syndrome), or a distal/nondominant vessel with low severity. Use the decision tree (Figure 2) to guide territory-specific recommendations.

bedside_protocol.title: Evaluate Imaging & Tissue At Risk
bedside_protocol.action: Confirm limited infarct core and favorable collateral status on CT/CTP or MRI. Assess for substantial salvageable tissue and consider perfusion mismatch if available.

bedside_protocol.title: Consider IV Thrombolysis & Time Window
bedside_protocol.action: Administer IV tPA if eligible. For EVT candidates, prioritize rapid treatment (<4.5 hours from onset when possible). Note that delays in reperfusion can reduce benefit in DMVO.

bedside_protocol.title: Select EVT Technique & Device
bedside_protocol.action: For M2 occlusions, an aspiration-first approach may be favorable. For distal/tortuous vessels, consider purpose-built adjustable stentrievers (e.g., Tigertriever 13) to minimize hemorrhagic complications. Use careful guidewire and microcatheter selection to reduce perforation risk.

bedside_protocol.title: Individualize Decision for Non-Standard Territories
bedside_protocol.action: For M3/M4, A2/A3, and P1-P3 occlusions, EVT is not routine. Consider only in cases with clearly disabling deficits (e.g., cortical blindness, dense hemiparesis, aphasia), large territory at risk, and favorable anatomy. Enroll eligible patients in ongoing DMVO trials (e.g., STEP MeVO, FRONTIER AP).

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Territory-specific, evidence-weighted recommendations incorporating the latest 2025-2026 RCT data (ESCAPE-MeVO, DISTAL, DISCOUNT, ORIENTAL-MeVO, DISTALS); practical decision tree for bedside use; addresses device selection and procedural safety.
- Limitations: Several trials had strict inclusion criteria leading to selection bias (e.g., exclusion of dominant M2, low median NIHSS); many recommendations rely on nonrandomized evidence and expert consensus due to neutral/negative trial results; publication of ORIENTAL-MeVO and DISTALS is still pending; generalizability limited by high rates of IV tPA in trial medical arms.
